item.dc:creator: friendlynoodles

item.title: A hexagonal storm with a diameter of 25,000 km raging at the north pole of Saturn
item.description: Saturn's hexagon is a persistent approximately hexagonal cloud pattern around the north pole of the planet Saturn, located at about 78°N. The sides of the hexagon are about 14,500 km (9,000 mi) long, which is about 2,000 km (1,200 mi) longer than the diameter of Earth The hexagon may be a bit more than 29,000 km (18,000 mi) wide, may be 300 km (190 mi) high, and may be a jet stream made of atmospheric gases moving at 320 km/h (200 mph). It rotates with a period of 10h 39m 24s, the same period as Saturn's radio emissions from its interior. The hexagon does not shift in longitude like other clouds in the visible atmosphere. Saturn's hexagon was discovered during the Voyager mission in 1981, and was later revisited by Cassini-Huygens in 2006. During the Cassini mission, the hexagon changed from a mostly blue color to more of a golden color. Saturn's south pole does not have a hexagon, as verified by Hubble observations. It does, however, have a vortex, and there is also a vortex inside the northern hexagon.[13] Multiple hypotheses for the hexagonal cloud pattern have been developed.
item.dc:creator: ddpaladiya

item.title: Laney Boggs - She's All That - Cosplay
item.description:
I have been told for years I look like Rachael Leigh Cook in this movie, so I caved and finally did the cosplay :P So here is me as Laney Boggs "The Nerd" and me as Laney Boggs the "Hot Chick".
Main references for this cosplay :D
item.dc:creator: Alsoashley

item.title: Tesla vs Unions
item.description: Just a friendly reminder that the conflict with Tesla in Sweden is still ongoing! :) Strikes and blockades since October - every now and then we get added measures like this company announcing they will no longer clear vehicle lifts for use (which can cause cascade problems if they potentially break the law by still using them etc). Unionize, we are stronger together! Original article at https://www.gp.se/ekonomi/unionen-varslar-om-blockad-vill-oka-trycket-pa-tesla.832bcc60-834d-4caf-b414-104cf0e6708e , just ran it through google translate.

Keep scrolling for cat tax

item.title: Dorothy
item.description: Dorothy Height, an American civil rights and womens’ activist, was once turned away from Barnard College in 1929, even after receiving an official acceptance letter. “I was accepted at Barnard College and was denied admission when I arrived because they had a quota of two (Negroes),” she said. “They did not know that I was not white…I was turned away.” She didn’t let that stop her. Instead, she took the subway downtown to NYU, Barnard acceptance letter in hand, and was accepted that same afternoon. “From that day forward, I have loved every brick of that university,” Height wrote in her memoir, Open Wide the Freedom Gates. She fought relentlessly for equality and justice and was so deeply involved with the Civil Rights Movement that she was often called upon to share advice on political issues. Her awards and recognitions are many, but one of the most powerful things she teaches us is that it’s entirely possible to take the one thing that tried to break you, and turn it into something powerful—for yourself and the greater good.
item.dc:creator: chickengood

item.title: That's a lot of fish
item.description: That is how you educate! That is how you include everyone. https://www.upworthy.com/elementary-school-learns-sign-language Young students at Nansemond Parkway Elementary School in Suffolk, Virginia, wanted to be able to communicate with the cafeteria worker who served them breakfast and lunch everyday. So they learned how. Leisa Duckwall is deaf and had been working at the school for four years serving the students and staff. Because Duckwall cannot hear, she and the students did the best they could to make it work, until a teacher had an idea. Kari Maskelony, who teaches fourth grade, spoke with Duckwall using American Sign Language (ASL) and noticed the cafeteria went silent. Students watched in awe as the two women used their hands to communicate. Maskelony grew up in a family that was hard of hearing, so sign language was part of her life, according to My Modern Met. After seeing the reaction of students, Maskelony asked the kids if they would like to learn the language.
item.dc:creator: Herebychoice
